children.value: Programming is the discipline that AI has changed first and fastest. Even students with no programming background can now write working code by describing what they want in plain English. For
coding — graphics, sound, generative art, interactive installations — the change is doubled: the model helps you write the code
This chapter is for everyone, programmers or not. We use programming as a creative medium and as a way to understand what is going on inside the tools we have been using.
By 2026 most professional development is done with an AI assistant in the editor. The dominant patterns are:
— the assistant suggests the next line or block as you type. Originated with GitHub Copilot in 2021.
— a sidebar that can see your code and respond in plain language. Tools:
— select code, press a shortcut, describe the change.
— describe a task, the assistant plans, edits multiple files, runs the code, fixes errors, and reports back. Tools:
For a beginner the most useful pattern is
commands. You write a draft (or paste an example), the assistant explains, you ask for changes, you iterate.
The assistant is fastest when you can read code. You do not need to write it from scratch.
it. Reading is what lets you supervise an AI.
A worked example: p5.js + an assistant

children.label: A worked example: p5.js + an assistant

children.value: is a JavaScript library descended from Processing, designed for visual sketches. You can use it directly in your browser at the
A useful prompt to an AI assistant, even if you have never seen JavaScript before:
“Modify this p5.js sketch so that instead of a single circle, twenty circles follow the mouse with a trailing delay, and their colour cycles through hues over time.”
A solid assistant will produce something like:
Run it; play with it; ask the assistant to “make the trail spring instead of linear”, “add a glow effect”, “double the number of circles”, “make it react to audio input”. The whole loop is
How to talk to a coding assistant

children.label: How to talk to a coding assistant

children.value: Tell it what kind of code you want.
.” “p5.js running in the browser.” Without that, the assistant defaults to whatever was most common in its training data.
Show it the smallest possible failing example.
“Explain this function line by line as if I have never seen JavaScript.”
“Write three small tests for this function.” Helps catch the mistakes the assistant cannot see.
Long conversations drift. Start a new chat for a new task.
The model is wrong more often in code than in prose — but it is also instantly checkable.
Generate placeholder assets with image and audio tools.
Wire them together in code with an AI assistant.
This is the modern equivalent of cardboard-prototyping a board game — fast, scrappy, generative.
Building a tiny AI-powered web tool

children.label: Building a tiny AI-powered web tool

children.value: By chapter 8 you should be able to build something like:
a web page where the user types a sentence and a generated image appears,
a sketch that listens to the microphone and reacts in colour,
a button that produces an AI-generated story riff,
a small dashboard that classifies your selected files into categories using a local model.
A clean stack for prototyping in 2026:
: HTML + a single JavaScript file, often using
, or backend-side via an API call.
All of this can be assembled with an AI assistant in an afternoon.
Build a sketch with an AI assistant

children.label: Build a sketch with an AI assistant

children.value: With your AI assistant of choice, build a
that meets at least two of:
colour changes with position or speed,
the canvas is responsive to window size.
Iterate at least three times: ask for changes, run, ask for more.
Save the sketch publicly and put the link in your weekly log.
Read code you did not write

children.label: Read code you did not write

children.value: Take the assistant’s longest function and
in your own words — in your log, line by line. (Use the assistant to check your explanation.) This habit is the most important coding skill in 2026.
Optional advanced track: a generative pipeline

children.label: Optional advanced track: a generative pipeline

children.value: a tiny script that wires them together.
For example: generate four images of “
” and a 20-second ambient track for each. Display all four with their soundscapes on a simple HTML page.
The assistant suggested a function. You ran it. It worked. Five minutes later you cannot remember what it does. What does that mean for your skill, and for the long-term reliability of your project?
What are three things a coding assistant is
at than a beginner? (Hint: novelty, debugging across abstraction, judging which library to use.)
Find one bug the assistant introduced in your sketch. How would you have found it without the assistant?
